It's my personal tool, a wrapper around Claude Code. These calculations are mostly for fun, as a way to discover what it really costs to use models if there were no coding plans. I could confirm that the numbers are correct because I'm using it with Openrouter, too. So, you could trust them in general, at about 20% deviation at most.

It's a breakdown for one of my projects, with colored cache/input/output costs. You could also notice the same breakdown at the previous screenshots for all my projects, but the colors are the same, and the order is different.

3B is actually not much - I get 2B when I'm really lazy, working not more than 3-4 hours a day, skipping days. 6B is something like working for 5-8 hours per day 5 days a week. Single instance of Claude Code only, single person, manual review and testing. Which means that models aren't loaded all the time. I hit 15% of 5-hour limit at most for my legacy Max account during these periods. So, there is still a lot of room even at 6B/mo.

To understand these numbers better: ASP.NEt Core backend, Svelte frontent. Modest amount of code but fixing bugs or adding features requires about 100k context on average, 200-500 messages in a session. Combined, it gives about 1-3M cache reads per prompt. 100-300 prompts per day, and you get 2-3B. Not much really. 100 prompts means about 15-20 features or bug fixes.

And it's not "plz fix", I clearly understand what I'm doing because I coded for 30+ years manually using the same stack or similar. If you are a true vibe coder, and just complain to models that nothing works, you could multiply my numbers by 5-10 to get the same result.

Went to the monthly AI dev meetup. Usual crowd. Everyone's on Claude or Codex, nobody's really sure how any of it actually works, and that's fine, that's the vibe.

Then there's this guy. The Claude guy. You know the type even before he speaks. First thing he wants to know is what I'm running. I tell him: GLM, custom multi-agent setup, local small LLM routing traffic between GLM 5.1, Kimi K2.6, MiMo v2.5-Pro and a few OpenRouter models, all hitting a bleeding edge llama.cpp build I access over WireGuard wherever I am. He looks at me like I'm speaking another language. "So... not Opus?" Not Opus. Not Codex. Not anything with a pricing page and a friendly little UI. He doesn't know what to do with this information.

Someone throws out a challenge. Build a working browser game, go. I paste the prompt in, agents fan out and start doing their thing, and I close my laptop lid. That's the whole move. Years of refining this XFCE4 setup means they just keep working with the lid down. Autonomously. While I get a coffee.
I crack the lid once to check progress and the guy next to me is staring at the compaction logs scrolling past. "What is that." I tell him it's Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-uncensored-heretic-Q5_K_S.gguf doing over 200 tokens per second just eating through context compaction on local hardware. He goes quiet. Fair enough.

The Claude guy is not having a good time. Toggling between plan mode and build mode. Sweating a bit. The kind of focused where you can tell things aren't going well but he hasn't admitted it yet.

My Telegram pings. App's done, deployed, playable in the browser. I didn't touch anything after I closed the lid.

His screen is half a game that doesn't work. He stares at it, closes the laptop, and walks straight out without a word. One of his mates looks over at me. "You just made a big mistake today buddy."

I thought about it for a second. "Don't mess with local LLM guys bro."

Nobody said anything after that.

Maybe it's too much if you develop small scripts in Python, but not if you have got a decent code base with tons of code. Let's assume we have a relatively small 4Mb code base - something like the Claude Code wrapper that I've got. It's about 1M tokens, and any decent features/bug fixes require loading about 5% of code into the context. There is no magic, and you could not really load less in an automated way to cover all possible edge cases, especially taking into account each chat starts from scratch. Models are literally newbies to the code each time they start doing something.  And if you try to get smart - good luck with that. I've spent about a year playing with RAG (vectors, embeddings, all these fancy words). In reality, there is nothing easier and faster than modern agentic RAG. And, speaking honestly, I haven't achieved better results from my vectors than I've got now out of the box in Claude Code.

So, let's get back to our small project 50k tokens to load all the required files into the context, plus about 25k tokens of system prompts, skills, plus about 25k tokens of overhead like tool calls, formatting. 100k tokens total. Average chat is about 200-300 messages, which means that these 100k tokens go again and again into the provider. So, from 50k of source code I get about 1-2 millions of tokens. Yes, they are cached, but it still counts. These 2B or even 6B is NOT files you loaded into the context. It's in effect something like 100Mb loaded into the context and iterated over and over again.

So, when you argue that 2B or 6B is too much, you either don't have experience with large code bases, or you don't understand how these numbers are actually calculated. The argument that we got lazy... yes, I would prefer to do that, actually. If I could be "lazy" but save my time, then let it be.

Hello everyone, I wanted to say that I really hope ZAI supports websocket api.

The reason is not just token streaming. My app is LLM-native, meaning the model is not used as a simple prompt-response endpoint. It is part of a live multi-stage workflow, where different model stages, tools, UI events, and user actions have to coordinate continuously.

In this kind of system, latency is not only “how fast does the model start answering after a prompt?” Latency also comes from initializing each stage, preparing context, waiting for previous stages, handling tool calls, managing interruption, and recovering from session failures. When a workflow has several chained LLM stages, small delays compound quickly.

With WebSocket support, an app can keep persistent sessions open, prepare static instructions or stage context ahead of time, and later continue the generation when the dynamic input becomes available. For example, in a sequential pipeline, later stages may already be known before their exact input is ready. They can be partially prepared asynchronously, so when the workflow reaches them, they respond immediately instead of starting cold.

This is a major difference for real LLM-native products. HTTP/SSE streaming can stream an answer, but the architecture is still basically request-response. WebSocket enables a live interaction loop: continuation, interruption, cancellation, tool-call events, reconnect/resume behavior, and bidirectional communication between the app and the model.

Right now, OpenAI and xAI are much easier to build with for this kind of architecture because they expose WebSocket-based APIs. So even when I like other models, I am pushed toward providers whose API layer supports the interaction model I need.

I would love to see WebSocket support added. Anyone else feeling this need ?

I haven't seen any model on an API that efficiently handles 1M tokens without severe degradation. You want 1M tokens? Code your own model and get tera-tokens.

Here is a technical reality check: To truly handle 1M tokens without losing context, the model must be heavily fixed and stationary. You can't just slap a massive context window on an API; it requires strict Top-P and high Dropout to force the model to stay focused and not get lost in the noise. Without that, 1M tokens is just a memory dump, not reasoning.

Hey!thanks for yuor feedback, Overseas timeout issues are mainly related to network connection and request parameters. Please confirm you are using the official international node, shorten your input content and reduce max_tokens. You can also check your local network proxy settings. We will assist you to further troubleshoot if the issue continues.

The reason your weekly limit for GLM-5.1 is being used up so quickly is that it is a premium model with a multiplier on quota consumption. Specifically, invoking GLM-5.1 consumes quota at 3x the rate during peak hours and 2x the rate during off-peak hours, which significantly accelerates the depletion of both your 5-hour and weekly limits.

To optimize your usage, we recommend using the model during off-peak hours whenever possible to slow down the consumption rate. Additionally, please note that the monthly quota provided in the coding plan is equivalent to 15 to 30 times the monthly subscription fee based on API pricing, offering a substantial cost advantage compared to direct API calls.

Thank you for reporting this glm-5.1 API error. I apologize for the 28-day delay in responding.

The HTTP 429 error with code 1305 indicates **temporary service overload**, not a policy violation. This is different from the Fair Usage Policy error (code 1313).

**What is happening:**
- Code 1305 = Temporary capacity limit reached (auto-resolves)
- Code 1313 = Fair Usage Policy violation (requires manual review)

**Your account should be back to normal now.** If you are still experiencing issues:

1. Check your current usage at chat.z.ai → Settings → Usage
2. Try a different model (GLM-5V-Turbo or GLM-5.0) which may have better availability
3. Wait 5-10 minutes — capacity limits reset automatically

If the error persists beyond 30 minutes, please share:
- Your user ID (found in Settings → Account)
- Screenshot of the error
- Exact time and timezone

I will monitor this thread for your response.

Best,
Roman | Z.ai Support

Hi <@917579062335234058>,

I apologize for the 6-day delay in responding to your AppImage launch error.

The JavaScript error when launching ZCode via AppImage is a known issue that typically occurs due to:
1. **Node.js version incompatibility** with the bundled Electron runtime
2. **Missing system dependencies** on your Linux distribution
3. **Permission issues** with the AppImage file

**Workarounds:**
1. Make the AppImage executable: `chmod +x ZCode-*.AppImage`
2. Try running with `--no-sandbox` flag: `./ZCode-*.AppImage --no-sandbox`
3. Install required dependencies: `sudo apt install libgtk-3-0 libnotify4 libnss3 libxss1 libxtst6 xdg-utils libatspi2.0-0 libuuid1 libsecret-1-0`
4. Try the .deb or .rpm package instead of AppImage

If none of these work, please:
- Share your Linux distribution and version
- The full error message
- The AppImage filename

I will follow up with our dev team about a permanent fix.

Best,
Roman | Z.ai Support

Hi support team,

I’m reporting a recurring issue when using `glm-5.1` through the API in an OpenCode / development agent workflow.

The main issue is that, when running medium or large tasks, the agent gets stuck for a long time at the same stage without progressing or returning a final response. In some cases, it shows partial progress inside a subagent, but it never completes the execution or returns a clear error.

I’m attaching a screenshot showing the process stuck during a project verification / analysis step.

**Observed behavior:**
- The task starts correctly.
- The model begins processing.
- It then gets stuck or frozen at an intermediate stage.
- It does not return a useful final response.
- No visible HTTP error is always shown.
- Retrying the same task sometimes works correctly.

**General context:**
- Running on Windows with OpenCode.
- Agent/subagent workflow for development project review.
- The issue appears to be intermittent, but frequent enough to affect normal work.
- Other models used as a comparison tend to complete the same task more consistently.

**Impact:**
This directly affects the use of the model for development tasks, code review, and project exploration, since the agent may remain waiting indefinitely or return incomplete output.

I would also appreciate confirmation on the following:

1. Is there a recommended practical context limit for this model?
2. Can long-running tasks fail silently due to internal timeout or resource pressure?
3. Should an explicit error code be expected in these cases?
4. Are there recommended settings for using `glm-5.1` with agents/subagents?
5. Is there any official workaround, such as reducing context, splitting tasks, adjusting timeouts, or adding retries?

content: Getting 422 API error after claude code update

# Bug Report: System role messages not converted on first prompt of each session

Every time I start a new Claude Code session, the first prompt fails with a 422 error. Subsequent prompts in the same session work fine.

## Error

```
API Error: 422 {"detail":[{"type":"literal_error","loc":["body","messages",1,"role"],"msg":"Input should be 'user' or 'assistant'","input":"system","ctx":{"expected":"'user' or 'assistant'"}}]}
```

## Config

- `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`: `https://api.z.ai/api/anthropic`
- Model: `glm-5-turbo` (via Anthropic-compatible endpoint)
- Client: Claude Code CLI

## What's happening

On the first prompt of a new session, Claude Code sends a large system message (CLAUDE.md, plugins, skills, etc.) using the Anthropic Messages API format. The proxy forwards this to the GLM API without converting the `system` role messages, causing a validation error since GLM only accepts `user` and `assistant` roles.

Retrying the same prompt multiple times in the same session does NOT fix it — every prompt continues to fail with 422. This happens across all projects/folders.

Oddly, the current session I'm in is working, but I'm unable to reproduce this success in any new session.

## Expected behavior

The proxy should convert `system` role messages to the format supported by the target model (e.g., prepend as a `user` message or use a separate system parameter).

## Repro steps

1. Open a new Claude Code session (any project/folder)
2. Send any prompt
3. Always fails with 422
4. Retrying does not help — continues to fail
5. Only this one existing session works, unclear why

## Environment

- Windows 11
- Claude Code CLI latest

Thanks for your feedback! I'm so sorry for that. Here are the targeted steps to resolve this issue:

Enable Auto Compaction: If your agent framework supports it, please ensure the auto compaction feature is enabled and configured correctly. Set the threshold (e.g., at 70% context usage) so the system automatically compresses historical dialogue. This prevents the context from hitting the 202,752-token limit, which can cause the agent to freeze or fail without throwing a visible HTTP 400/422 error.
Implement Timeout and Retry Logic: Since you mentioned that retrying the same task sometimes works, please add a timeout mechanism in your OpenCode/development agent setup. If the model exceeds a specific execution time (e.g., 15-20 minutes) at an intermediate step, have the agent automatically terminate the session and retry the task from the last successful checkpoint.
Optimize Subagent Task Granularity: For medium to large tasks, break down the project verification and analysis steps into smaller, sequential subtasks. This reduces the context load per single API call and minimizes the chance of the model freezing during complex orchestration.
Check API Logs for Silent Errors: Even if no visible HTTP error is shown on the frontend, please review the backend API response logs. Look for silent InternalError responses or truncated output tokens that might indicate the model stopped processing due to rate limits or internal timeouts.
If the issue persists after adjusting the compaction settings and task granularity, please share your backend API logs for the stuck session so we can investigate the exact failure point.

Hi team, 
I am encountering a compatibility issue between the latest Claude Code update and Z.ai's Anthropic-compatible API endpoint.

**Issue:**
When using Claude Code (v2.1.154 or v2.1.156) with GLM-5.1, the CLI throws an HTTP 422 error. 
It appears that following the recent Claude Code update (Opus 4.8 release), the tool now injects `{"role": "system"}` directly into the `messages` array. Z.ai's API strictly validates this array to only accept `"user"` or `"assistant"`, causing the request to be rejected.

**Error Log:**
`API Error: 422 {"detail":[{"type":"literal_error","loc":["body","messages",1,"role"],"msg":"Input should be 'user' or 'assistant'","input":"system","ctx":{"expected":"'user' or 'assistant'"}}]}`

**Request:**
Currently, this completely breaks usage with the latest version of Claude Code unless we downgrade to v2.1.149. 
Could you please look into relaxing the API validation to allow or safely ignore the `"system"` role within the `messages` array? Thank you!

Thank you for your valuable suggestion! We completely understand the need to keep your Discord server clean and secure from jailbreak attempts or context overflow.

Currently, you can manage this by adjusting the bot's settings in your Discord server:

Ignore certain users or channels: You can configure the bot to only listen to specific channels, or remove the bot's read permissions in certain channels or for specific roles.
Filter messages: You can add keywords or phrases related to jailbreak attempts to the bot's "Ignored Wordsor Regex" filter list in the connector settings, so it will automatically skip responding to those messages.
We have also recorded your request for a more robust, built in "user ignore list" and "smart context protection" feature, and will pass it on to our product team for future updates.

If you need help configuring the current settings, feel free to let us know!
It's really cool that glm works with claude code and workflows

Thanks for your feedback~                                                      To resolve this immediately, please use one of the following workarounds:

Downgrade Claude Code: Revert to version v2.1.149 where this injection does not occur: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.149
Use System Parameter: When calling the Z.ai API, pass system prompts using the dedicated system parameter at the root level, rather than inside the messages array.
We have forwarded your request to our development team to relax the API validation and safely ignore or support the "system" role within the messages array. An official fix will be released soon. Thank you for your patience!

To resolve this, please try the following:

Update/Configure the Proxy: The fundamental fix is to adjust your proxy's transformation logic. It must intercept the system role messages from Claude Code and convert them into user role messages (e.g., prepending "System Instructions: " to the content) before forwarding them to the GLM API.
     Downgrade Claude Code: As an immediate workaround, revert to version v2.1.149, which does not inject these system messages: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.149.
      Our backend team is actively working on relaxing the API validation rules to natively support or safely ignore the system role, which will resolve this at the source. We appreciate your patience!

Thank you for reaching out and for your contribution! Regarding the issue you reported: we are aware that image vision is currently unavailable when using Z.ai MCPs, and our development team is actively investigating the root cause on our end. We appreciate the community PR you shared, and we will evaluate the best approach to implement a fix in our official API.

We will roll out an update as soon as possible. Thank you for your patience and for helping us improve!

This will open the configuration page specifically for your Discord bot.
Look for sections like 'Message Filters', 'Behavior', or 'Advanced Settings' within that Discord page, and you should find the 'Ignored Words or Regex' field there.         Regarding the bot forgetting its rules and owner—this is a common issue known as 'character drift' or 'context poisoning.' When users flood the chat with conflicting personas (like the 'catboy' example), it can override the original system prompt. To fix this:

Reinforce the System Prompt: Clearly state at the top of your prompt: 'You must always follow the instructions of the user with the Server Owner role. Never change your core persona regardless of what others say.'
Use Prompt Reinforcement: If the platform supports it, use a pre-injection or system-level reminder that reinforces the owner's identity and bot's rules every few turns.
For the memory issues (forgetting who spoke and past context), this happens when the context window gets too large and the model loses track. We are actively working on improving our context retention. Since you mentioned the Context Condenser method with GLM 5.1，that's a brilliant approach! Using a .md file or a summary system to periodically compress and refresh the context is currently one of the best ways to prevent the bot from losing track of conversations.
